article_title: Webb Finds Interstellar Comet 3I/ATLAS May Be 10 to 12 Billion Years Old, With 30 Times the Deuterium of Solar System Comets
summary: Accurate, well-hedged Webb 3I/ATLAS isotope/age follow-up; every claim, quote, number and date verified against the four cited fetchable sources. Sole warning is a non-blocking allowlist note (earthsky.org); no factual issue to correct.

content_preview.title: Webb Finds Interstellar Comet 3I/ATLAS May Be 10 to 12 Billion Years Old, With 30 Times the Deuterium of Solar System Comets
content_preview.summary: James Webb's NIRSpec spectrograph measured isotopic ratios in interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS as it left the inner solar system, pointing to an origin during the universe's 'cosmic noon.'
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The third interstellar object ever observed passing through the solar system has yielded a new clue to its origin. As comet 3I/ATLAS moved away from the Sun, astronomers turned the James Webb Space Telescope's Near-Infrared Spectrograph (NIRSpec) on it and measured isotopic ratios that differ sharply from anything seen in comets native to our own system. According to [NASA Science](https://science.nasa.gov/missions/webb/nasas-webb-finds-clues-to-ancient-distant-origin-of-comet-3i-at...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (764 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Clear Overview / What We Know / What We Don't Know / Analysis arc. Technical depth (deuterium-to-hydrogen ratio as a frozen-in formation fingerprint, carbon-13 enrichment over galactic time) is explained accessibly without overreaching.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 4 source snapshots read from disk and verified (gunzipped). source-0 (NASA Science, 200): confirms 'NIRSpec revealed exceptionally high levels of deuterium, about 30 times more than seen in solar system comets' verbatim, the Cordiner quote verbatim with correct attribution (astro-chemist, NASA Goddard, Greenbelt MD, lead author), 'began moving away from the Sun in December 2025', and NIRSpec detection of water/CO2/CO. source-1 (ESA, 200): confirms 'showed only traces of carbon-13 compared to lighter-weight carbon-12' verbatim, 'began moving away from the Sun in December 2025' verbatim, and paper 'published on 22 June 2026 in the journal Nature'. source-2 (EarthSky, 200): confirms '10 to 12 billion years ago, during the universe's cosmic noon, when star formation was at its height' verbatim and the Milam quote verbatim. source-3 (Sci.News, 200): confirms 'The abundance of heavy water shows that 3I/ATLAS spent its formative years in a deeply frozen state' verbatim and 'Cyrielle Opitom of the University of Edinburgh ... UV-Visual Echelle Spectrograph (UVES) on ESO's Very Large Telescope between December 6 and 26, 2025' verbatim. No WebFetch fallback needed; all snapshots returned 200.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every specific traces to a cited fetchable source: deuterium ~30x (NASA/ESA), traces of carbon-13 vs carbon-12 (ESA/NASA), 10-12 Gyr age and 'cosmic noon' (EarthSky/NASA/ESA), Dec 2025 timing (NASA/ESA), VLT/UVES Opitom Dec 6-26 2025 (Sci.News). Rule 9 satisfied: the Nature paper (DOI 10.1038/s41586-026-10771-6) is deliberately NOT in the sources array and NO substantive claim rests on it; the only mention is the publication fact ('published June 22 in the journal Nature'), which is itself carried verbatim by the NASA and ESA snapshots. The article's '4.6 billion years ago' for the solar system vs the sources' '4.5 billion' is hedged with 'roughly' and is uncontroversial common knowledge.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and measured. No sensationalism despite a striking result. The age claim is consistently hedged: title 'May Be', body 'could have formed', 'estimate', and an explicit 'What We Don't Know' section noting it is 'a model-dependent inference, not a direct measurement' and that the host system is unidentified.
editor_notes.originality: Genuine follow-up, not a duplicate. The only prior 3I/ATLAS coverage is the 2026-03-01 prebiotic-chemistry article, which the piece correctly cross-references; the internal link (/article/2026-03/01-interstellar-comet-3iatlas-reveals-prebiotic-chemistry-as-it-heads-for-jupiter-encounter) resolves to an existing article whose subject (water, methane, coma chemistry) matches the cross-reference description. This article centers on the new isotope/age finding (where and when the comet formed), a distinct angle.
recommendations: earthsky.org is not on config/source_allowlist.txt (non-blocking warning). It is a reputable, established astronomy outlet and every EarthSky-attributed claim is independently corroborated by the NASA and ESA primary sources, so no factual risk. Maintainer may consider adding earthsky.org to the allowlist; per review policy I did not modify the allowlist file.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. Accurate, neutral, appropriately hedged interstellar-comet follow-up. All four cited sources personally read and confirm every quote, number, name and date. No claim rests on the cookie-walled Nature paper. No orphan URLs (all 4 body URLs are in article.sources). The script's APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS default was driven solely by the non-blocking allowlist warning, which is not a correctable factual error; verdict set to APPROVE with no corrections record.
